M.S. THESIS· UNIVERSITY OF ULSAN GRADUATE SCHOOL· DEPT. OF BIOMEDICAL SCIENCES

Predicting sepsis six hours
before it happens

A causal Transformer that learns the shape of physiological deterioration across four independent ICU databases — built to work even when hospitals define sepsis differently.

Yeeun ChoiAdvisor: Prof. Hangsik ShinAug 2026
0.879
AUC, internal validation (step‑wise, 6h pre‑onset)
0.717/0.718
AUC, external validation on 2 unseen datasets
~105k
ICU stays across 4 international databases
29
routinely‑available variables — no rare labs required
↓ scroll to explore the study
CH.01 — OVERVIEW

A disease that moves faster than the chart can be reviewed

Sepsis kills through delay. The clinical evidence is clear on what to do — the hard part is knowing early enough who needs it.

19.7%
of all deaths worldwide involve sepsis — roughly 49 million cases and up to 21 million deaths a year.
42%
in‑hospital mortality for sepsis that develops inside the ICU, versus 26.7% hospital‑wide (WHO, 2020).
3–7%
rise in mortality risk for every hour antibiotics are delayed after suspected onset.
10.1%
of Korean sepsis cases actually received bundle treatment within the recommended 1‑hour window (53.6% within 3h).

International guidelines (Surviving Sepsis Campaign) call for a “bundle” of blood cultures, broad‑spectrum antibiotics, and fluids within 1–3 hours of suspected sepsis. In practice, ICU staffing and the sheer speed of deterioration make that window hard to hit — and blood cultures themselves take up to 48 hours to confirm, far too slow to serve as a real‑time trigger.

A stream of early‑warning models — EWS, qSOFA, and more recent deep‑learning systems — have tried to close that gap. Reviewing this literature surfaces two recurring failure modes that block real clinical adoption:

GAP 01

Single‑center training, no external validation

Most published models are trained and tested inside one hospital system (often MIMIC‑III alone). Performance quietly collapses when the demographic mix, staffing pattern, or equipment changes.

GAP 02

No shared definition of “sepsis onset”

SIRS, SOFA, and ICD codes each draw the line differently, and SIRS in particular doesn’t reliably indicate sepsis at all. Studies built on different ground truths can’t be benchmarked against each other.

THIS STUDY’S RESPONSE
01

Four independent databases

One domestic (AMC) and three US sources (MIMIC‑IV, PhysioNet, eICU) are pooled for training, then the model is external‑validated twice on data it never saw.

02

Multiple diagnostic criteria at once

Sepsis‑3 and Adult Sepsis Event (ASE) onset logic are harmonized across databases with different fields available, so the model isn’t locked to one institution’s definition.

03

A forecasting‑safe architecture

A causally‑masked Transformer only ever looks backward in time — the same constraint a real bedside deployment would face — while targeting a 6‑hour early‑warning horizon.

CH.02 — METHOD

Four hospital systems, one shared feature set

Every design choice below exists to make the model portable: the same 29 inputs, the same causal constraint, regardless of which hospital’s EHR it runs on.

2.1  Data

DatabaseSettingCollection periodSepsis casesAnalytic ICU staysRole in this study
AMCAsan Medical Center, South KoreaSingle center, ward + ICU2011–202049,75529,475Train / internal val.
MIMIC‑IVBIDMC, United StatesSingle center, ward + ICU2008–202231,91023,688Train / internal val.
PhysioNet ABIDMC, United StatesSingle center ICUPhysioNet/CinC 20191,790 / 20,33620,335Train / internal val.
PhysioNet BEmory Univ. Hospital, USSingle center ICUPhysioNet/CinC 20191,142 / 20,00019,997External val. #1
eICU‑CRD208 hospitals, United StatesMulticenter ICU2014–201512,204 admissions11,608External val. #2

AMC + MIMIC‑IV + PhysioNet set A are pooled and split 80/20 for training and internal (hold‑out) validation. PhysioNet set B and eICU‑CRD are held out entirely for two independent external validations — the second specifically testing generalization to a 208‑hospital population the model never trained on.

Onset definition, harmonized

Sepsis onset is anchored to a suspected infection time: the earlier of a qualifying blood‑culture order and antibiotic prescription, combined with the Sepsis‑3 SOFA‑change rule (or ASE criteria for AMC). Because each database records different fields — eICU, for instance, has too few culture records to apply the rule directly — the study adapts a published proxy (multiple antibiotics within 72h) so all four sources can be reduced to the same onset logic.

29 shared variables

3 demographics (age, sex, length of stay) + 7 vital signs (HR, RR, SBP, DBP, MAP, temperature, SpO₂) + 19 labs, all chosen because they’re available across every database — three chemistry values were dropped for inconsistent cross‑site distributions. No rare or manually‑charted fields (e.g. GCS) are required.

2.3–2.4  From raw records to a model‑ready tensor

2.5  Model architecture

An MTS‑Transformer encoder classifies, at every hour, whether sepsis will begin within the next 6 hours — using only information available up to that point.

Handling imbalanceInverse‑frequency class weights [0.537, 7.176] + label smoothing (0.11) so the model isn’t over‑confident at fuzzy onset boundaries.
TuningOptuna (TPE), 50 trials maximizing validation AUC → 128‑dim embeddings, 4 heads, 10 layers, batch 82, AdamW @ 1e‑5.
Leak preventionCausal attention masking — at any timestep the model cannot see the future, mirroring real bedside deployment.

2.6  Two ways of grading the same model

STEP‑WISE

Scores every individual hour as positive if it falls 0–6h before onset. This is the standard AUC/AUPRC classification view used throughout Result §3.1.

ALARM‑WISE

Asks the more clinical question: does an hourly alarm actually fire inside a valid 6–24h pre‑onset window? A COMPOSER‑style snooze policy then silences repeat alarms for a set duration to fight alarm fatigue.

2.7  Explaining the model — DeepSHAP

DeepLIFT‑based DeepSHAP attributes each prediction back to its 29 inputs at every timestep, using up to 100 training patients as the background distribution — producing a time × feature contribution matrix per patient (Result §3.4).

CH.03 — RESULT

Performance that survives leaving the hospital it was trained in

Three validation slices, one held‑out in time and two held‑out by institution — plus an interactive look at how the model’s risk score actually moves as a patient approaches onset.

INTERNAL VALIDATION
0.879
AUC · step‑wise, 6h pre‑onset
AUPRC 0.447
20% hold‑out of AMC + MIMIC‑IV + PhysioNet A
EXTERNAL VALIDATION #1
0.717
AUC · step‑wise, 6h pre‑onset
AUPRC 0.052
PhysioNet set B — unseen hospital (Emory)
EXTERNAL VALIDATION #2
0.718
AUC · step‑wise, 6h pre‑onset
208 hospitals
eICU‑CRD — fully unseen multicenter database

AUPRC drops on the highly imbalanced external sets, as expected — but AUC stays well above chance on both, and training/validation/internal‑test performance stayed close throughout, indicating the model isn’t simply memorizing its training distribution.

Full ROC & precision–recall curves

The step‑wise metrics above, plotted in full — ROC on the left of each panel, precision–recall on the right, with 95% bootstrap confidence bands.

How far in advance can it see?

Re‑labeling the internal test cohort (n=5,766 patients with long enough sequences) at 12h, 18h, and 24h before onset shows graceful, not catastrophic, degradation the further out the target moves.

Try it — the risk trajectory as it plays out

This panel reconstructs the qualitative pattern reported in the thesis (Fig. 8): non‑sepsis patients stay under the model’s decision threshold, while the sepsis cohort’s average risk score climbs steadily and crosses threshold around 6 hours before onset. Curves are an illustrative simulation built from the reported statistics (threshold = 0.64, crossing point = −6h) — not a re‑export of patient‑level data.

TIME TO ONSET
−6 h
RISK SCORE
0.640
ALARM STATE
ARMED
−48honset →+6h

Turning it into an alarm: the snooze trade‑off

Firing an alert every hour a threshold is crossed is exhausting for staff. Silencing repeat alarms for a “snooze” window (COMPOSER‑style) trades a little sensitivity for a lot less noise — and the thesis found a sweet spot at 3–6 hours. Values below approximate the reported range (AUC 0.780–0.807, peak at 3–6h); per‑hour points are reconstructed, not digitized source data.

ALARM‑WISE AUC
0.807
Baseline (no snooze, 1h alarms): AUC 0.785

What is the model actually looking at?

DeepSHAP attributions peak in the hours right before onset, cresting around 6h pre‑onset before fading — matching the model’s own early‑warning horizon.

Ranked by mean |SHAP| across the cohort:

ALSO RANKED HIGH
  • #7 Hemoglobin
  • #8 Arterial pH
  • #9 Calcium
  • #12 Chloride
  • #14 Platelet count
  • #15 White blood cell count

Length of stay and blood‑pressure variables dominate — clinically coherent, since falling blood pressure is a core sepsis sign. Rate‑of‑change features (e.g. respiratory‑rate trend) rank highly too, showing the model reads trajectories, not just snapshots.

Where this sits next to prior systems

SystemSepsis definitionVariables usedReported AUCExternal validation
This studySepsis‑3 + ASE (multi‑criteria)290.879 internal · 0.717–0.718 external2 independent sets
COMPOSERSepsis‑3Not fully disclosed≈0.85–0.94 (patient‑level TP)Single site, prospective
TREWSSepsis‑3Not fully disclosedOutcome‑linked, not AUC‑matchedMulti‑site, prospective
DeepSEPSSepsis‑3≈30 (incl. GCS)0.778–0.80Not reported here
Epic Sepsis Model v2Sepsis‑3≈800.75–0.864 institutions

Evaluation designs differ enough (alarm‑vs‑patient‑level positives, different time horizons) that these numbers aren’t a strict head‑to‑head benchmark. The practical distinction this study leans on: fewer, more commonly‑available variables, and native support for more than one diagnostic definition.

CH.04 — DISCUSSION

What this adds up to, and what it doesn’t prove yet

Read as a case report rather than a monitor readout: what the model demonstrates, where it’s still limited, and what a clinical team would need to see next.

STRENGTHS
  • Pools four independent ICU databases across two countries and validates externally twice, directly addressing the single‑center weakness common to prior sepsis‑prediction literature.
  • Trains and evaluates across multiple concurrent diagnostic criteria (Sepsis‑3, ASE) instead of committing to one institution’s definition — every criterion tested still cleared AUC 0.7.
  • Uses only 29 routinely‑collected variables, versus roughly 80 in some comparable systems and none requiring manually‑charted scores like GCS — a real practical edge for deployment.
  • DeepSHAP attributions line up with known sepsis physiology — falling blood pressure, rising respiratory‑rate variability, acid‑base and electrolyte disturbance — which is a meaningful sanity check on why the model works, not just that it does.
LIMITATIONS
  • Retrospective only. Nothing here shows that firing this alarm actually lowers mortality, speeds up antibiotics, or improves outcomes in practice — that requires a prospective trial.
  • Label uncertainty at the boundary. Using several diagnostic criteria improves generality, but onset time isn’t always unambiguous for every patient; label smoothing softens this but doesn’t remove it.
  • EHR data quality is inherited, not solved. Missingness, shifting charting practice, and manual‑entry error were mitigated with clinically‑grounded outlier rules — but not eliminated. Prospective, standardized data collection would address this at the source.
CONCLUSION

A causally‑masked Transformer, trained across AMC, MIMIC‑IV, PhysioNet, and eICU, predicts sepsis 6 hours before onset with AUC 0.879 internally and 0.717–0.718 across two external institutions it never trained on. Predicted risk rises steadily as onset approaches, and an hourly alarm built on that score reaches AUC 0.785–0.807 depending on snooze policy — evidence that a lightweight, criteria‑agnostic early‑warning tool built from routinely‑available ICU variables can help clinicians flag high‑risk patients and start treatment inside the golden hour, pending prospective validation.

CITATION

Choi, Y. (2026). Transformer Model for Early Sepsis Prediction in ICUs: Development and Multicenter External Validation. M.S. Thesis, University of Ulsan Graduate School, Dept. of Biomedical Sciences. Advisor: Prof. Hangsik Shin.

Data sources acknowledged: Asan Medical Center · MIMIC‑IV (PhysioNet) · eICU‑CRD (PhysioNet) · PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2019.

ABOUT THIS PAGE

Built as an independent explainer derived from the thesis above. The interactive trajectory and snooze charts are illustrative reconstructions of reported statistics for demonstration purposes, not exports of the original patient‑level data. Not intended for clinical use.
